metadata.title: Automation Is Different This Time

beats.narration_text: med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: In 1979, GM employed 800,000 workers and generated 11 billion dollars of revenue. In 2012, Google generated 14 billion dollars with 58,000 workers — one-fourteenth the workforce for more revenue. Every previous wave of automation created more jobs than it destroyed. The new wave may skip that step.

Historical automation: farm machinery replaced farm workers who moved to factories. Industrial automation replaced factory workers who moved to services. In each wave, the new sector absorbed more workers than the old sector lost.

production_viz.label: Historical absorption pattern: agriculture → factories → services — each wave creates more jobs than destroyed

beats.narration_text: What specific metric reveals that this wave of automation is structurally different from previous ones — and why can't the historical pattern repeat?

card.copy: Agriculture → factories → services: each wave created more jobs than it destroyed. What's different about the information economy?

beats.narration_text: Job intensity: workers per million dollars of revenue. GM in 1979: 800,000 workers, 11 billion dollars — 73 workers per million. Google in 2012: 58,000 workers, 14 billion dollars — 4 workers per million. 18 times less labor-intensive.

production_viz.label: Job intensity bar chart: GM 1979 at 73 workers/$M vs Google 2012 at 4 workers/$M — 18× gap

beats.narration_text: Netflix in 2012 replaced Blockbuster. Blockbuster at peak employed 84,000 workers. Netflix employed 4,500. The new business providing the same service employed 1 in 18 of the workers. Not a temporary transition effect — a permanent structural difference.

production_viz.label: Netflix 4,500 vs Blockbuster 84,000 employees — same service, 1/18 the workers — structural not transitional

beats.narration_text: Total US work hours: 194 billion hours in 1998 and in 2013 — essentially flat over 15 years. During the same period, worker productivity rose 42 percent and the US population grew by 40 million. More people, more output, same total hours. The jobs were not created.

production_viz.label: US 1998-2013: productivity +42% diverging from flat hours worked at 194B — population +40M annotated

beats.narration_text: The previous pattern required new sectors to be labor-intensive. Farm to factory: many factory jobs. Factory to services: many service jobs. Services to information: few information jobs. The replacement sector employs vastly fewer people.

production_viz.label: Sector transition job intensity: farm→factory (high), factory→services (high), services→information (18× lower)

beats.narration_text: This doesn't automatically mean unemployment — people find other work. But it means the transition is no longer self-correcting. The new sector doesn't absorb the displaced workers. Something else must fill the gap.

production_viz.label: Absorption gap diagram — displaced workers from old sector vs small absorptive capacity of information sector

beats.narration_text: The historical automation optimism rested on a pattern that has now broken. If information-age industries produce 18 times less employment per dollar, and they are replacing all other industries, the net employment effect requires active policy, not just market dynamics.

production_viz.label: Historical absorption pattern broken — information economy 18× less labor-intensive replacing all sectors

beats.narration_text: Job intensity: GM 1979 at 73 workers per million dollars. Google 2012 at 4 workers per million dollars. 18 times difference. Netflix replaced Blockbuster with 1 in 18 the workers. US 1998 to 2013: hours flat, productivity up 42 percent, population up 40 million. The historical absorption pattern is broken.

card.copy: GM 1979: 73 workers/$M. Google 2012: 4 workers/$M. 18× gap. Netflix vs Blockbuster: 4,500 vs 84,000. US hours flat 1998–2013. Information economy doesn't absorb displaced workers.

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
